source_paper_title: Sparse Integer Programming Is Fixed-Parameter Tractable
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Eisenbrand_2025, title={Sparse Integer Programming Is Fixed-Parameter Tractable}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0162}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0162}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Eisenbrand, Friedrich and Hunkenschröder, Christoph and Klein, Kim-Manuel and Koutecký, Martin and Levin, Asaf and Onn, Shmuel}, year={2025}, month=aug, pages={2141–2156} }
context:
Section 7 (Concluding remarks), page 27

In Section 5, we have given a structural characterization of matrices $A$ with $g_1(A)$ bounded by showing that $g_1(A)$ is bounded if and only if $A$ is row-equivalent to a matrix with small dual tree-depth and small entry complexity. Unfortunately, a similar (if and only if) characterization of matrices $A$ with $g_{\infty}(A)$ bounded does not seem to be in our reach.
Problem 1. Find a structural characterization of matrices $A$ with $g_{\infty}(A)$ bounded.
title: Characterize matrices with bounded Graver basis infinity norm under row operations
background_latex:
Let $A\in \mathbb{Q}^{m\times n}$ be a rational matrix. The integer kernel of $A$ is
\[\ker_{\mathbb{Z}}(A)=\{x\in \mathbb{Z}^n: Ax=0\}.\]
For $x,y\in \mathbb{Z}^n$, write $x\sqsubseteq y$ if (i) $x$ and $y$ lie in the same orthant, i.e., $x_i y_i\ge 0$ for all $i\in [n]$, and (ii) $|x_i|\le |y_i|$ for all $i\in [n]$. The Graver basis $\mathcal{G}(A)$ is the set of $\sqsubseteq$-minimal nonzero vectors in $\ker_{\mathbb{Z}}(A)$.
Define
\[ g_{\infty}(A)=\max\{\|x\|_{\infty}: x\in \mathcal{G}(A)\},\qquad \|x\|_{\infty}=\max_{i\in [n]} |x_i|.\]
Two matrices $A,A'\in \mathbb{Q}^{m\times n}$ are row-equivalent if one can be obtained from the other by elementary row operations (multiplying a row by a nonzero scalar, or adding a scalar multiple of one row to another). Row-equivalent matrices have the same integer kernel, hence the same Graver basis and the same value of $g_{\infty}(\cdot)$.
A \emph{structural characterization} here means a description, in terms of identifiable structural properties/invariants of $A$ (e.g., graph- or matroid-theoretic parameters associated with $A$, or existence of a row-equivalent matrix with certain sparsity/depth/encoding properties), that is equivalent to the boundedness of $g_{\infty}(A)$ over the class under consideration.
problem_definition_latex:
Find conditions on a rational matrix $A\in\mathbb{Q}^{m\times n}$ that are equivalent to the boundedness of $g_{\infty}(A)$, i.e., characterize the class of matrices for which there exists a function $F$ (depending only on the characterized structural parameters) such that
\[ g_{\infty}(A) \le F. \]
Equivalently, provide an if-and-only-if structural description of those $A$ for which $g_{\infty}(A)$ is bounded (as a finite number) by a bound determined solely by the proposed structural invariants.
latex:
Let $A\in \mathbb{Q}^{m\times n}$ be a rational matrix. The integer kernel of $A$ is
\[\ker_{\mathbb{Z}}(A)=\{x\in \mathbb{Z}^n: Ax=0\}.\]
For $x,y\in \mathbb{Z}^n$, write $x\sqsubseteq y$ if (i) $x$ and $y$ lie in the same orthant, i.e., $x_i y_i\ge 0$ for all $i\in [n]$, and (ii) $|x_i|\le |y_i|$ for all $i\in [n]$. The Graver basis $\mathcal{G}(A)$ is the set of $\sqsubseteq$-minimal nonzero vectors in $\ker_{\mathbb{Z}}(A)$.
Define
\[ g_{\infty}(A)=\max\{\|x\|_{\infty}: x\in \mathcal{G}(A)\},\qquad \|x\|_{\infty}=\max_{i\in [n]} |x_i|.\]
Two matrices $A,A'\in \mathbb{Q}^{m\times n}$ are row-equivalent if one can be obtained from the other by elementary row operations (multiplying a row by a nonzero scalar, or adding a scalar multiple of one row to another). Row-equivalent matrices have the same integer kernel, hence the same Graver basis and the same value of $g_{\infty}(\cdot)$.
A \emph{structural characterization} here means a description, in terms of identifiable structural properties/invariants of $A$ (e.g., graph- or matroid-theoretic parameters associated with $A$, or existence of a row-equivalent matrix with certain sparsity/depth/encoding properties), that is equivalent to the boundedness of $g_{\infty}(A)$ over the class under consideration.

Find conditions on a rational matrix $A\in\mathbb{Q}^{m\times n}$ that are equivalent to the boundedness of $g_{\infty}(A)$, i.e., characterize the class of matrices for which there exists a function $F$ (depending only on the characterized structural parameters) such that
\[ g_{\infty}(A) \le F. \]
Equivalently, provide an if-and-only-if structural description of those $A$ for which $g_{\infty}(A)$ is bounded (as a finite number) by a bound determined solely by the proposed structural invariants.
open_problem_quote:
In Section 5, we have given a structural characterization of matrices $A$ with $g_1(A)$ bounded by showing that $g_1(A)$ is bounded if and only if $A$ is row-equivalent to a matrix with small dual tree-depth and small entry complexity. Unfortunately, a similar (if and only if) characterization of matrices $A$ with $g_{\infty}(A)$ bounded does not seem to be in our reach.
Problem 1. Find a structural characterization of matrices $A$ with $g_{\infty}(A)$ bounded.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally generate families of matrices and compute $g_{\infty}(A)$ for small sizes (via Graver basis computation) to test candidate characterizations, but the search space is large and Graver basis computation scales poorly, limiting brute-force counterexample hunting to small instances.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate structural characterizations can be encoded as computable features (graph/matroid parameters, depth measures, sparsification via row operations), with a fitness signal based on agreement with computed $g_{\infty}(A)$ on sampled instances. However, computing $g_{\infty}(A)$ and relevant invariants may be expensive, and the space of plausible characterizations is broad and hard to parameterize tightly.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The forward-citation set provided does not contain a solution to the requested row-operation-invariant characterization of bounded \(g_\infty(A)\). The most substantial partial progress is the 2023 arXiv work “Characterization of matrices with bounded Graver bases and depth parameters and applications to integer programming”, which develops a matroid-theoretic framework for row-equivalence-invariant sparsification and depth minimization. It proves exact equalities \(\mathrm{td}^*_P(A)=\mathrm{dd}(M(A))\), \(\mathrm{td}^*_D(A)=\mathrm{c}^*\!\mathrm{d}(M(A))\), and \(\mathrm{td}^*_I(A)=\mathrm{c}^*\!\mathrm{dd}(M(A))+1\), thereby giving structural characterizations (in terms of the column matroid \(M(A)\)) of when there exists a row-equivalent matrix of bounded primal/dual/incidence treedepth. It also shows that \(g_1(A)\) is bounded if and only if the circuit \(\ell_1\)-complexity \(c_1(A)\) is bounded, equivalently iff \(A\) is row-equivalent to a matrix with bounded dual treedepth and bounded entry complexity; this yields new preconditioning algorithms and FPT results parameterized by \(g_1(A)\) or \(c_1(A)\).

For \(g_\infty(A)\), however, the same paper explicitly isolates the gap: it formulates the open problem of characterizing matrices with bounded \(g_\infty(A)\) and demonstrates that bounded incidence treedepth (even very small, e.g. 4) does not imply bounded \(g_\infty(A)\) by exhibiting a family whose Graver basis contains \((t-1,1,\dots,1)\). Thus, naive attempts to extend the \(g_1\)-characterization by simply replacing dual treedepth with incidence treedepth fail. The other citing works contribute algorithmic tools and highlight which structural regimes suffice for bounding Graver-related quantities: bounded primal treedepth plus bounded coefficient size yields bounds on \(g_\infty(A)\), while bounded dual treedepth plus bounded coefficients yields bounds on \(g_1(A)\). They also emphasize that \(\ell_\infty\)-proximity and \(g_\infty\)-type invariants can be intrinsically large in dual-treedepth regimes, suggesting that any eventual characterization of bounded \(g_\infty(A)\) will likely require invariants beyond (or different from) treedepth alone, potentially combining matroidal structure with coefficient/denominator control or new notions of “primal-like” decomposability preserved under row operations.

sources.title: (Near)-Optimal algorithms for sparse separable convex integer programs
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper gives near-linear-time algorithms for separable convex integer programs with sparse/treedepth-structured constraint matrices. In particular:
- If \,\(\mathrm{td}_P(A)\) and \,\(\|A\|_\infty\) are bounded, then (IP) can be solved in time \,\(g(\mathrm{td}_P(A),\|A\|_\infty)\, n\,\log(\max(\|u-l\|_\infty,\|b\|_\infty))\) (Theorem 1).
- If \,\(\mathrm{td}_D(A)\) and \,\(\|A\|_\infty\) are bounded, then (IP) can be solved in time \,\(g(\mathrm{td}_D(A),\|A\|_\infty)\, n\,\log n\,\log(\max(\|u-l\|_\infty,\|b\|_\infty))\) (Theorem 2).
Technically, it proves sensitivity/proximity-type bounds in \(\ell_1\) controlled by \(g_1(A)=\max_{g\in\mathcal G(A)}\|g\|_1\): changing bounds by \(\delta\) changes an optimum by at most \(\|\delta\|_1 g_1(A)\) (Theorem 5), and for certain ‘partially scaled’ variants it bounds \(\|x_I-x_J\|_1\le 4|I\triangle J|\, g_1(A)\) (Theorem 6).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for an if-and-only-if structural characterization (up to row operations) of when the Graver basis infinity norm \(g_\infty(A)\) is bounded. This paper does not attempt such a characterization and does not provide necessary-and-sufficient structural conditions for bounded \(g_\infty(A)\) under row equivalence.

However, it is relevant because it:
- Uses Graver-basis parameters \(g_p(A)\) (notably \(g_1(A)\)) and conformal proximity bounds \(P_p(A)\) as central complexity drivers.
- Relates algorithmic performance for treedepth-structured matrices to bounds on \(g_\infty(A)\) and \(P_\infty(A)\) (primal case) and to \(g_1(A)\) (dual case), and cites known bounds such as \(g_1(A)\le (2\|A\|_\infty)^{2^{O(\mathrm{td}_D(A))}}\).
- Highlights a key obstacle: for bounded \(\mathrm{td}_D(A)+\|A\|_\infty\), \(\ell_\infty\)-proximity to the LP optimum can be \(\Omega(n)\), so bounding \(g_\infty(A)\) (or \(P_\infty(A)\)) is not the right invariant in that regime.

Thus the paper contributes tools/insights about which structural parameters (treedepth, \(\|A\|_\infty\)) imply computable bounds on Graver-related quantities, but it does not characterize all matrices with bounded \(g_\infty(A)\).

sources.title: Circuit and Graver walks and linear and integer programming
sources.theorem_or_result:
Main algorithmic results:
- Theorem 1.1: For an LP \(\max\{wx: Ax=b,\ l\le x\le u\}\), a circuit walk from any feasible point \(x_0\) to an optimal point can be computed in polynomial time using linear algebra plus solving the given LP once.
- Theorem 1.2: For an IP \(\max\{wx: x\in\mathbb Z^n, Ax=b,\ l\le x\le u\}\), a Graver walk from any feasible point can be computed in time polynomial in the bit-size of \(w,l,u\) and in \(\Delta(A)\) (given in unary) using an integer-programming oracle.
- Theorem 3.6: For fixed \(a,d\), Graver walks are polynomial-time computable over matrices with \(\Delta(A)\le a\) and \(\min\{\mathrm{td}(A),\mathrm{td}(A^\top)\}\le d\).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for an if-and-only-if structural characterization (up to row operations) of when the Graver basis infinity norm \(g_\infty(A)\) is bounded by a function of structural invariants. This paper does not attempt such a characterization and does not provide necessary-and-sufficient conditions for bounded \(g_\infty(A)\).

However, it is tangentially related in two ways:
- It uses Graver elements and known bounds on Graver elements in terms of \(\Delta(A)\) (e.g., \(\|g\|_1\le (\mathrm{rank}(A)+1)\Delta(A)\) for primitive circuits/Graver elements), and it leverages recent “sparse IP is FPT” results (bounded tree-depth + bounded subdeterminants) to obtain polynomial-time computability of Graver walks.
- It highlights structural regimes (bounded tree-depth and bounded \(\Delta(A)\)) where Graver-based augmentation is tractable, which are examples of matrix classes where Graver complexity measures (including norms) are controlled, but it does not relate these to row-equivalence-invariant characterizations of bounded \(g_\infty(A)\).

sources.title: A Brief History of Parameterized Algorithms for Block-Structured Integer Programs (Invited Talk)
sources.theorem_or_result: This citing paper is an invited-talk survey/review and does not prove a new structural characterization of matrices with bounded Graver basis infinity norm under row operations. It recalls (from earlier works such as [73], [31]) that for matrices with bounded coefficient size \(\|A\|_\infty\) and bounded treedepth (primal or dual), one can bound Graver norms: \(g_\infty(A)\) is bounded by a function of \(\|A\|_\infty\) and \(\mathrm{td}_P(A)\), and \(g_1(A)\) is bounded by a function of \(\|A\|_\infty\) and \(\mathrm{td}_D(A)\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an if-and-only-if structural characterization (row-operation invariant) of when \(g_\infty(A)\) is bounded in terms of structural parameters. This survey notes sufficient conditions (bounded \(\|A\|_\infty\) plus bounded primal treedepth implies bounded \(g_\infty\)) and points to related characterization work (it cites [11] “Characterization of matrices with bounded graver bases and depth parameters…”), but it does not itself establish an equivalence or a full characterization. It therefore provides context and pointers rather than progress on the characterization problem.
